{{Short description|Quantum mechanical principle: two identical fermions cannot occupy the same quantum state}}
{{Infobox physical law
| name = Pauli exclusion principle
| image = Wolfgang Pauli.jpg
| caption = [[Wolfgang Pauli]], who formulated the principle in 1925
| formula = <math>\Psi(\ldots, \mathbf{r}_i, s_i, \ldots, \mathbf{r}_j, s_j, \ldots) = -\Psi(\ldots, \mathbf{r}_j, s_j, \ldots, \mathbf{r}_i, s_i, \ldots)</math>
| discovered_by = [[Wolfgang Pauli]] (1925)
}}

The '''Pauli exclusion principle''' is the [[quantum mechanics|quantum mechanical]] principle which states that two or more [[identical particles|identical]] [[fermion]]s (particles with half-integer [[Spin (physics)|spin]]) cannot occupy the same [[quantum state]] within a quantum system simultaneously. This principle was formulated by Austrian physicist [[Wolfgang Pauli]] in 1925.

The principle applies to all [[fermion]]s, which include [[electron]]s, [[proton]]s, [[neutron]]s, [[neutrino]]s, and [[quark]]s.

== Mathematical formulation ==
For a system of identical fermions, the total wave function must be [[antisymmetric]] with respect to exchange of any two particles:
:<math>\Psi(\mathbf{r}_1, s_1, \ldots, \mathbf{r}_i, s_i, \ldots, \mathbf{r}_j, s_j, \ldots) = -\Psi(\mathbf{r}_1, s_1, \ldots, \mathbf{r}_j, s_j, \ldots, \mathbf{r}_i, s_i, \ldots)</math>

This antisymmetry implies that if two fermions occupy the same state (same spatial orbital AND same spin), the wave function vanishes identically.

In the [[Slater determinant]] formalism, this is automatically enforced: the determinant of a matrix with two identical rows is zero.

== Spin–statistics theorem ==
{{Main|Spin–statistics theorem}}
The exclusion principle is a consequence of the [[spin–statistics theorem]]:
* '''Fermions''' (half-integer spin): antisymmetric wave function → obey exclusion principle
* '''Bosons''' (integer spin): symmetric wave function → can occupy the same state (e.g., [[Bose–Einstein condensate]]s)

== Applications ==

=== Electronic structure of atoms ===
The exclusion principle explains the [[periodic table]]. No two electrons in an atom can have the same four quantum numbers (<math>n, \ell, m_\ell, m_s</math>). This forces electrons to fill successive [[atomic orbital|orbitals]], creating the shell structure of atoms.

=== Stability of matter ===
The exclusion principle prevents electrons from collapsing into the [[atomic nucleus]]. Without it, all matter would collapse into an ultra-dense state. As [[Freeman Dyson]] and others showed, the stability of bulk matter is fundamentally a consequence of the exclusion principle.

=== Degeneracy pressure ===
{{Main|Degenerate matter}}
In [[white dwarf]]s and [[neutron star]]s, gravitational collapse is resisted by:
* '''Electron degeneracy pressure''' — supports white dwarfs ([[Chandrasekhar limit]]: <math>M \leq 1.4 M_\odot</math>)
* '''Neutron degeneracy pressure''' — supports neutron stars

=== Solid-state physics ===
The exclusion principle underlies the [[electronic band structure]] of solids:
* [[Conduction band]] and [[valence band]] formation
* Distinction between [[metal]]s, [[semiconductor]]s, and [[insulator (electricity)|insulator]]s
* [[Fermi energy]] <math>E_F</math> — the highest occupied energy level at <math>T = 0</math>

== See also ==
{{Portal|Physics}}
* [[Fermion]]
* [[Spin–statistics theorem]]
* [[Quantum number]]
* [[Hund's rules]]

== References ==
{{Reflist|30em}}

[[Category:Pauli exclusion principle| ]]
[[Category:Quantum mechanics]]
[[Category:Wolfgang Pauli]]
